Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd tell myself I'd study in the evenings, but then I'd just end up scrolling on my phone or watching something instead. When I did study, it was usually late at night when guilt finally kicked in, and I'd cram through whatever was most urgent. I know I should have been more organized, but I just couldn't find the energy to stick to any kind of schedule.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really driven by panic more than planning. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then suddenly realize a deadline was coming and pull a late-nighter. Regular study times felt like something other students did, not me. I always had good intentions at the start of each week, but they'd fall apart by Tuesday.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline notification or seeing classmates stress about an assignment that made me log in. Sometimes I'd get an email reminder and think, okay, I should probably check what's happening. I rarely logged in just to explore or stay ahead. It was always reactive, never proactive.
Q4: Not really, no. There were maybe a few weeks where I tried to check in regularly, but it never became automatic for me. The LMS always felt like something I had to force myself to use rather than something that fit naturally into my day. I'd open it, feel overwhelmed by what I saw, and close it again.
Q5: The videos were actually helpful when I watched them because I'm a visual learner and they broke things down clearly. I watched quite a few hours of them, usually in one sitting when I was trying to catch up. The gamified tasks they introduced were kind of fun too, they made me feel like I was accomplishing something even when my motivation was low.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums. Posting there felt pointless and awkward, like who's even reading this stuff? I also skipped a lot of the supplementary readings because they felt like extra work when I was already struggling to do the basics. If it wasn't directly tied to an assignment grade, I probably didn't touch it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it counted toward my grade. The gamified tasks were good because I could see progress and earn points, which gave me a small dopamine hit. Anything that felt abstract or disconnected from actual assessment just seemed like wasted effort to me.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist in my mind. If it was optional, I'd convince myself I'd get to it later, but later never came. Required activities at least had some external pressure attached, so I'd eventually do them. Optional activities needed me to have internal motivation, and honestly, I just didn't have much of that this semester.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs, then study from them on my tablet without being online. Sometimes I'd rewatch videos I'd saved or look things up on YouTube when the course explanations didn't click. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS, just me with my notes and some coffee.
Q10: There were nights where I'd spend hours going through downloaded materials or rewriting notes by hand to understand concepts better. None of that showed up anywhere. I'd also think through problems while walking or on the bus, trying to make sense of things in my head. The LMS only saw me when I was clicking around, not when I was actually processing information.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, then use it repeatedly offline over the following days. It was easier than logging in each time, especially when my internet was being slow or unreliable. One download could fuel several study sessions that the system never knew about.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially mid-semester when everything piled up and I felt like I was already too far behind to catch up. I'd feel this heavy sadness and just not want to deal with any of it. My attendance dropped because showing up felt pointless when I didn't understand what was going on anyway.
Q13: Absolutely. I had part-time work and not a lot of money, so I was always tired and stressed about other things. When I was exhausted from a shift, the last thing I wanted was to log into the LMS and face more demands. Fatigue made everything feel harder than it probably was, and I'd just avoid it entirely.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd be logged in and clicking through things but my brain was somewhere else entirely. I'd watch a video but realize at the end I hadn't absorbed anything because I was too tired or distracted. Those sessions felt like going through the motions without actually learning.
Q15: All the time. I'd know an assignment was important but I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, then tomorrow became next week. I think I avoided things because starting felt overwhelming, and the longer I waited, the more overwhelming it got. It was a vicious cycle of avoidance and guilt.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd see a deadline approaching and finally kick into gear, usually the night before. Without that external pressure, I'd just drift. The LMS probably shows spikes of activity right before due dates, which is basically my entire engagement pattern.
Q17: Feedback helped when I actually got it, but sometimes it came too late to change anything. If I got encouraging feedback, I'd feel a brief boost of motivation. Negative feedback just made me feel worse and less likely to engage. It's hard when you're already feeling low and then get told your work isn't good enough.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but it assumed students were keeping up consistently, which I wasn't. Once I fell behind, it was hard to know where to jump back in. The gamified elements helped a bit because they broke things into smaller, achievable chunks, but overall I felt lost in the structure.
Q19: The gamified tasks actually made engagement easier because they felt less intimidating than big assignments. But the discussion requirements were harder because I didn't feel confident contributing when I wasn't fully caught up. Having clearer pathways for students who'd fallen behind would have helped me a lot.
Q20: I think those students might be like me, clicking around trying to figure out what's going on, maybe starting things but not finishing them. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes you're just lost and searching, or you're procrastinating by looking busy without actually doing the work.
Q21: That kind of describes me in a way. My LMS activity was low, but I did enough offline studying to pass my exams and complete most assignments. I'm not proud of my engagement level, but I managed to scrape by through bursts of focused work that didn't show up in the system.
Q22: Logs can show when you're online and what you click, but they can't show if you're actually thinking or learning. They miss all the offline work, the mental processing, the conversations with friends about course content. They also can't capture emotional states, like whether you're engaged or just going through the motions.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it, not just pass. It means showing up mentally, not just physically or digitally. For me, real engagement happened in rare moments when something clicked and I felt curious instead of just obligated. Those moments were few this semester.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally, or how much effort it took just to do the minimum. It wouldn't show the nights I spent feeling sad and unmotivated, or the guilt I carried about not doing better. It also wouldn't show that the gamified tasks actually helped lift my mood a bit, even if my overall engagement looked low.
